images.caption:
Figure 1:Training loss on a 10B MoE model trained for 200B FineWeb-Edu tokens.
Both asynchronous runs remain stable throughout training, with Async PP + Error Feedback closing the final gap to the synchronous baseline entirely.

Figure 2:Validation loss of synchronous and one-step delayed optimizers on the 360M model.
For most optimizers, Error Feedback cuts the sync-async gap by more than half compared to standard delayed training and outperforms the synchronous-start baseline.

Figure 3:Synchronous and delayed AdamW and Muon training on the 360M model.
AdamW degrades substantially under one-step delay, even with a synchronous start, whereas Muon achieves a much smaller final sync-async gap.

Figure 4:Final loss gap between synchronous and asynchronous training as a function of the momentum decay for various optimizers.Note that we exclude certain high-momentum configurations in which the synchronous baseline itself diverges due to instability.

Figure 5:Validation loss of the 2B MoE model across training horizons.
The synchronous and asynchronous scaling curves remain nearly parallel from5050B to200200B tokens, indicating no growth of the sync-async gap with longer training; Error Feedback consistently reduces the remaining gap.

Figure 6:Comparison of synchronous training, PipeDream-2BWwith constant one-step delay, and the original PipeDream schedule with variable delay on the 135M model.
HerePPdenotes the number of pipeline stages in the original PipeDream schedule; PipeDream-2BWhas a constant one-step delay independent ofPP.
AsPPincreases, original PipeDream progressively degrades relative to the corresponding PipeDream-2BWruns.
This illustrates that variable delay remains a major source of degradation at larger pipeline depths.

header: Appendix AHyperparameter Sensitivity Results and Other Additional Experiments

images.caption:
Figure 7:Effect of peak learning rate on synchronous and one-step delayed training on the 135M model.
Bars show final validation loss, and annotations indicate the sync-async gap.
Lower learning rates mildly reduce the gap for robust optimizers, while large learning rates can substantially worsen delayed training for AdamW.

Figure 8:Effect of weight decay on synchronous and one-step delayed training on the 135M model.
Moderate values have limited effect for robust optimizers, while extreme values can substantially worsen delayed training.
AdamW is especially sensitive to small weight decay in this sweep.

Figure 9:Effect of learning-rate warmup length on one-step delayed training on the 135M model.
The delay is enabled from the first step in all runs.
Longer warmup mildly reduces the sync-async gap, with the largest visible effect for AdamW.

Figure 10:Effect of gradient clipping threshold on synchronous and one-step delayed training on the 135M model.
The sync-async gap is largely insensitive to the clipping threshold for the robust optimizers tested here.

Figure 11:Effect of learning-rate scheduler on synchronous and one-step delayed training on the 135M model.
The gap is similar across the tested schedules for Muon, NorMuon, and SOAP.
Some AdamW bars are clipped for readability; annotations show the corresponding sync-async gap.

Figure 12:Effect of the second-moment or variance decay coefficientβ2\beta_{2}on synchronous and one-step delayed training on the 135M model.
Unlike the primary momentum coefficient,β2\beta_{2}does not produce a consistent optimizer-independent trend.
Some bars are clipped for readability; annotations show the corresponding sync-async gap.

Figure 13:Two-dimensional batch-size versus peak-learning-rate sweeps on the 135M model.
For each optimizer, we show synchronous validation loss, one-step delayed validation loss, and the sync-async gap.
Smaller batch sizes substantially reduce the gap, but do not necessarily yield the best absolute asynchronous loss; larger batch sizes can produce large gaps, but also degrade the synchronous baseline.

Figure 14:Final validation loss versus the Taylor correction coefficientλ\lambdafor DC-ASGD-style compensation on SmoLLM-135M with Muon.
None of the tested coefficients improves over the standard delayed baseline.

Figure 15:Final validation loss as a function of the Error-Feedback coefficientλ\lambdaon the 135M model.
The valueλ=0\lambda=0corresponds to standard delayed training without Error Feedback, whileλ=1\lambda=1is the default correction used in the main experiments.
The dependence onλ\lambdais optimizer-specific: NorMuon shows a clear U-shape with an optimum below11, while AdamW, Muon, and SOAP perform best at somewhat larger values in this sweep.
Dotted lines show the corresponding synchronous baselines.
Figure 16:Loss as a function of learning rate for synchronous and delayed 2B MoE training at different scales.
(a)Full training dynamics.
(a)Full training dynamics.
(b)Zoomed-in view around delay start.
(a)Full training dynamics.
(a)Full training dynamics.
(b)Zoomed-in view around delay start.

Figure 19:Gradient-level Error Feedback on the 135M model.
Unlike the update-level correction used in the main experiments, applying the correction directly to raw gradients leads to divergence in our setting.
Figure 20:The relationship between theβ2\beta_{2}value and the loss “spike”.
